comment_text.snp4.12:1.3: Here, as often in the Aṭṭhakavagga, jānāti implies different ways of knowing, which may be right or wrong (to degrees). We can “see” things differently, but we can’t “know” them differently.
comment_text.snp4.12:3.2: Preferring omako over mago (“beast”), which seems harsh.
comment_text.snp4.12:5.1: I think “this is correct” refers, not to the assertion of a view, but to the assertion that the other is a fool. Niddesa takes it to mean the assertion of one of the 62 views, but this seems to be reading into the text.
comment_text.snp4.12:5.2: Here I take bālā as the quoted speech, as in the lines above and below. The commentary supplies the expected quotation indicator: ‘bālo’ti āhu. If we take bālā as subject of the verb here, per Bodhi, Norman, and Ñāṇadīpa, then the Buddha is adopting the reductive language of those he seeks to counter, obscuring the sense of the verse.
comment_text.snp4.12:5.3: It’s important to maintain the past sense of akaṁsu here: a view is something they have constructed and built up over time; it has solidified and become part of them, which is why it’s so hard to give up.
comment_text.snp4.12:7.2: The readings and interpretation of this line is tricky; see discussions in Norman and Bodhi. Niddesa and Arthapada are in agreement, and I follow.
comment_text.snp4.12:9.2: The Niddesa glosses aññatra saññāya niccaggāhā, which is translated by Bodhi as “apart from the grasping of permanence by perception”. But this seems impossible to me: saññāya is an ablative constructed with aññatra, and niccāni surely agrees with saccāni. Further, the sense of this reading is a stretch, as we haven’t dealt with impermanence at all so far. I think the allusion is, rather, to the idea of dhammaniyāmatā, the “ﬁxity” or “regularity” of the Dhamma as an “eternal truth”.
comment_text.snp4.12:10.1: This verse begins a rather striking pattern where the first couplet is essentially restated in the second couplet.
comment_text.snp4.12:11.1: Again the two couplets repeat the same meaning.
comment_text.snp4.12:13.3: This must refer back to the previous verse, where sayaṁ means “on one’s own account”.
comment_text.snp4.12:17.2: Here uddhaṁ has the sense “further, all the more”.

root_text.snp4.12:1.3: Yo evaṁ jānāti sa vedi dhammaṁ,
root_text.snp4.12:12.4: Diṭṭhī hi sā tassa tathā samattā.
root_text.snp4.12:13.3: Atha ce sayaṁ vedagū hoti dhīro,

translation_text.snp4.12:0.2: The Shorter Discourse on Arrayed For Battle
translation_text.snp4.12:1.2: those who are skilled disagree, arguing:
translation_text.snp4.12:1.3: ‘Whoever sees it this way <j>understands the teaching;
translation_text.snp4.12:1.4: those who reject this are inadequate.’
translation_text.snp4.12:2.2: saying, ‘The other is a fool, an amateur!’
translation_text.snp4.12:2.3: Which one of these speaks true,
translation_text.snp4.12:2.4: for they all claim to be an expert?”
translation_text.snp4.12:3.2: makes you a useless fool lacking wisdom,
translation_text.snp4.12:3.3: then they’re all fools lacking wisdom,
translation_text.snp4.12:3.4: for they all maintain their own view.
translation_text.snp4.12:4.1: But if having your own view makes you pristine—
translation_text.snp4.12:4.2: pure in wisdom, expert and intelligent—
translation_text.snp4.12:4.3: then none of them lack wisdom,
translation_text.snp4.12:4.4: for such is the view they have all embraced.
translation_text.snp4.12:5.1: I do not say that it is correct
translation_text.snp4.12:5.2: when they call each other fools.
translation_text.snp4.12:5.3: Each has built up their own view to be the truth,
translation_text.snp4.12:5.4: which is why they take the other as a fool.”
translation_text.snp4.12:6.1: “What some say is true and correct,
translation_text.snp4.12:6.2: others say is hollow and false.
translation_text.snp4.12:6.4: why don’t ascetics say the same thing?”
translation_text.snp4.12:7.1: “The truth is one, there is no second,
translation_text.snp4.12:7.2: understanding which folk would not dispute.
translation_text.snp4.12:7.3: But those ascetics each boast of different truths;
translation_text.snp4.12:7.4: that’s why they don’t say the same thing.”
translation_text.snp4.12:8.1: “But why do they speak of different truths,
translation_text.snp4.12:8.2: these proponents who claim to be skilled?
translation_text.snp4.12:8.3: Are there really so many different truths,
translation_text.snp4.12:8.4: or do they just follow their own lines of reasoning?”
translation_text.snp4.12:9.1: “No, there are not many different truths
translation_text.snp4.12:9.2: that, apart from perception, <j>are lasting in the world.
translation_text.snp4.12:9.3: Having formed their reasoning <j>regarding different views,
translation_text.snp4.12:9.4: they say there are two things: true and false.
translation_text.snp4.12:10.1: The seen, heard, or thought, <j>or precepts or vows—
translation_text.snp4.12:10.2: based on these they show disdain.
translation_text.snp4.12:10.4: saying, ‘The other is a fool, an amateur!’
translation_text.snp4.12:11.1: They take the other as a fool on the same grounds
translation_text.snp4.12:11.2: that they speak of themselves as an expert.
translation_text.snp4.12:11.3: Claiming to be an expert on their own authority,
translation_text.snp4.12:11.4: they disdain the other while saying the same thing.
translation_text.snp4.12:12.1: They are perfect, <j>according to their own extreme view;
translation_text.snp4.12:12.2: drunk on conceit, imagining themselves proficient.
translation_text.snp4.12:12.3: They have anointed themselves in their own mind,
translation_text.snp4.12:12.4: for such is the view they have embraced.
translation_text.snp4.12:13.1: If the word of your opponent makes you deficient,
translation_text.snp4.12:13.2: then they too are lacking wisdom.
translation_text.snp4.12:13.3: But if on your own authority you’re a knowledge master, <j>an attentive one,
translation_text.snp4.12:13.4: then there are no fools among the ascetics.
translation_text.snp4.12:14.1: ‘Those who proclaim a teaching other than this
translation_text.snp4.12:14.2: have fallen short of purity, and are inadequate’:
translation_text.snp4.12:14.3: so say each one of the sectarians,
translation_text.snp4.12:14.4: for they are deeply attached to their own view.
translation_text.snp4.12:15.2: denying that there is purification in other teachings.
translation_text.snp4.12:15.3: Thus each one of the sectarians, being dogmatic,
translation_text.snp4.12:15.4: speaks forcefully within the context <j>of their own journey.
translation_text.snp4.12:16.1: But in that case, so long as they are speaking forcefully <j>of their own journey,
translation_text.snp4.12:16.2: how can they take the other as a fool?
translation_text.snp4.12:16.3: They are the ones who provoke conflict
translation_text.snp4.12:16.4: when they call the other a fool <j>with an impure teaching.
translation_text.snp4.12:17.1: Standing in judgment, <j>measuring by their own standard,
translation_text.snp4.12:17.2: they dispute with the world more and more.
translation_text.snp4.12:17.3: But a personage who has given up all judgments
translation_text.snp4.12:17.4: creates no conflict in the world.”

variant_text.snp4.12:3.2: Bālomako → bālo mago (bj, sya-all, pts-vp-pli1, mr)
variant_text.snp4.12:4.3: parihīnapañño → koci nihīnapañño (bj, sya-all); kocipi nihīnapañño (mr) | te → nānāto (mr)
variant_text.snp4.12:14.2: suddhimakevalī te → suddhimakevali te (bj); suddhimakevalīno (si, pts-vp-pli1)
variant_text.snp4.12:16.3: medhagamāvaheyya → medhagaṁ āvaheyya (bj, sya-all); medhakaṁ āvaheyya (pts-vp-pli1)

reference_text.snp4.12:3.1: csp1ed18.416, csp2ed18.416, dr25.549, ms18Sn_1059, pts-vp-pli172, vnp880, vns887
